message.content:
Adversarial refuter: ASSUME this finding is FALSE; disprove it from the code at /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay. survives=true only if you genuinely cannot.
CV-O-1: PII/secret redaction is bypassed for every non-.jsonl transcript file (operator email + username already leaked into committed transcripts/)
scripts/capture_transcripts.py:104
`_copy_scrubbed` only runs the `_scrub` redaction when `srcf.suffix == ".jsonl"` (line 104-105). Every other captured file type — `.json`, `.meta.json`, `.txt` (tool-results), `.js` (workflow scripts) — falls to the `else` branch and is copied byte-for-byte verbatim (`data = raw`, line 107). The redaction patterns added in hardening passes (#F-024 home-path/email masking, #G-007 bare-username, #H-018 project-dir slug, plus the credential-shaped secret patterns) therefore never run on the bulk of the captured corpus.

The pre-commit hook (.githooks/pre-commit) `git add`s the result of this script, and the script's own docstring states the repo may be published. The bypass is not theoretical: the already-committed transcripts/ tree contains, in non-.jsonl files only, 54,299 unredacted `/Users/[REDACTED] path occurrences (json:2487, js:36, txt:51776 — zero in .jsonl, proving the scrub works there and is skipped elsewhere), 82 occurrences of `[REDACTED]`, and 10 of `[REDACTED]` — exactly the operator identity #F-024/#G-007 were written to mask. .jsonl files show 0 leaks, confirming the gate is the cause, not the patterns.

This turns a stated, hardened security/provenance control into a silent no-op for the file types that dominate the snapshot, and has already published operator PII to the tracked tree.
PoC: cd /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay && python3 -c "import re,glob,collections; pat=re.compile(r'/Users/[REDACTED]'); c=collections.Counter(); [c.update({f.split('.')[-1]: len(pat.findall(open(f,errors='ignore').read()))}) for f in glob.glob('transcripts/**/*',recursive=True) if not f.endswith('.jsonl')]; print('unredacted by ext:',{k:v for k,v in c.items() if v}); print('jsonl leaks:', sum(len(pat.findall(open(f,errors='ignore').read())) for f in glob.glob('transcripts/**/*.jsonl',recursive=True)))"
# -> unredacted by ext: {'json':2487,'js':36,'txt':51776}; jsonl leaks: 0
# emails: grep -rho '[REDACTED]' transcripts --include='*.txt' | wc -l  -> 82

attachment.content:
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.text: The PoC has a directory bug, but the email/path grep confirms leaks. Let me fix the PoC to skip directories and confirm the per-extension breakdown and that jsonl is clean.

input.command:
cd /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay && python3 -c "
import re,glob,os,collections
pat=re.compile(r'/Users/[REDACTED]'); c=collections.Counter()
for f in glob.glob('transcripts/**/*',recursive=True):
    if os.path.isfile(f) and not f.endswith('.jsonl'):
        c[f.split('.')[-1]] += len(pat.findall(open(f,errors='ignore').read()))
print('unredacted by ext:',{k:v for k,v in c.items() if v})
jl=sum(len(pat.findall(open(f,errors='ignore').read())) for f in glob.glob('transcripts/**/*.jsonl',recursive=True))
print('jsonl leaks:', jl)
"

content.text:
All claims are verified against the live code and repository state.

- **Code gate confirmed**: `capture_transcripts.py:104-107` — `_scrub` runs only when `srcf.suffix == ".jsonl"`; the `else` branch sets `data = raw` (verbatim copy). The capture loop (lines 138-143) feeds all non-`.jsonl` file types from the session subtree through `_copy_scrubbed`, so `.json`, `.txt`, `.js` are all copied unredacted.
- **Leaks confirmed in tracked tree**: per-extension counts match the finding exactly — `{'json': 2487, 'js': 36, 'txt': 51776}` unredacted `/Users/[REDACTED] occurrences, **0** in `.jsonl` (proving the patterns work and the suffix gate is the sole cause). 82 `[REDACTED]` and 10 `[REDACTED]` occurrences present.
- **Tracked & publishable**: `git ls-files` shows 1252 non-`.jsonl` files committed under `transcripts/`; these are the leaking files.

I attempted to disprove the finding and could not. The suffix check is a real bypass, the redaction patterns exist and demonstrably work on `.jsonl` only, and operator PII is already committed to the tracked tree in the unscrubbed file types.

input.reasoning: Verified true; could not disprove. Code: /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay/scripts/capture_transcripts.py:104-107 — _scrub() is invoked only when srcf.suffix == ".jsonl"; the else branch copies bytes verbatim (data = raw). The capture loop (lines 138-143) routes ALL non-.jsonl session-subtree files (.json, .meta.json, .txt tool-results, .js workflow scripts) through _copy_scrubbed, so redaction never runs on them. The _scrub function (lines 80-91) contains the hardened patterns (#F-024 path/email, #G-007 bare username, secret patterns) and demonstrably works — repo scan shows 0 unredacted /Users/[REDACTED] occurrences in .jsonl files, proving patterns are correct and only the suffix gate causes the bypass. Tracked-tree leaks confirmed exactly as claimed: 2487 (json) + 36 (js) + 51776 (txt) = 54299 unredacted /Users/[REDACTED] occurrences; 82 occurrences of [REDACTED] and 10 of [REDACTED]. git ls-files shows 1252 non-.jsonl files committed under transcripts/, and the pre-commit hook git-adds this output for a repo the docstring says may be published. The control is a silent no-op for the dominant file types and operator PII is already published.

content.text: The finding CV-O-1 is verified as true. I've completed the adversarial refutation task and submitted the structured output. No task list changes are needed — this refutation work is part of the in-progress hardening campaign pass and doesn't warrant a separate task entry.
